SEN. CORY BOOKER (D-NJ) IS INTERVIEWED ON MSNBC

APRIL 2, 2025

SPEAKERS:

SEN. CORY BOOKER (D-NJ)

ARI MELBER, HOST: I would say the man of the hour, but he might be the man of 25 hours.

Senator Cory Booker, thanks for joining me on "The Beat" tonight.

SEN. CORY BOOKER (D), NEW JERSEY: No, thank you. And there's an extraordinary man that mans this camera here for the — for your network, and he gave me a seat. So I want to bless him for that.

MELBER: You deserve a seat probably. Senator, we've set aside a good amount of time here because so many people want to hear from you. I have to be honest with you. We have not set aside 25 hours.

BOOKER: Ha! I'm grateful for that. That's a blessing too.

MELBER: We've heard you and we've heard why you did this. Would you be willing to share what you've heard back from your constituents, from citizens? Because now you've had some time to digest the response.

BOOKER: Not as much. My staff has sort of been dosing out some things to me as I've tried to, you know, just physically recover, even though my spirit is really soaring.

MELBER: Sure.

BOOKER: The gratitude from people whose voices I've elevated, who — who write to their senator with really personal, painful stories, to know that those people were in some ways affected by the fact that I read their letters and told their stories and talked to their pain and anger, that relieves me. I just want to make sure that I'm always respecting people who tell me about their challenges with Parkinson's or dementia, or their challenges with disabled children.

But to hear from Americans that feel really ignited and inspired, especially those who aren't that political or not that kind of base partisanship, that was really what I was dreaming of, is that somehow I could strike a chord that wasn't the normal left-right debates, but that in many ways could lift us beyond those to talk about a larger moral moment.

MELBER: We showed some of the international reaction, which, again, is not how the Senate, let alone the Democrats in the minority, have been heard from over the last months. You — you changed that. Here's some more of the political reaction we've seen.

(BEGIN VIDEO CLIP)

FRANK LUNTZ, POLLSTER & POLITICAL ANALYST: What Cory Booker did over the last 24 hours may have changed the course of political history. But he struck the kind of tone that grassroots Democrats are looking for.

CHARLAMAGNE THA GOD, CONTRIBUTOR, 'THE DAILY SHOW': A Democrat actually doing something, wow! I mean, wow, that's the longest someone's held Congress captive since January 6th.

SUNNY HOSTIN, CO-HOST, 'THE VIEW': We need that kind of energy coming from the Democrats. People feel that no one is fighting for them. And when I look at Cory Booker, I feel like Cory Booker is fighting for them.

(APPLAUSE)

(END VIDEO CLIP)

MELBER: We have some sense of what moved you to do this substantively, because we got to hear you explain it. I am curious, why now? Why this moment? You did it going into those elections, not knowing the outcome. You did it after some of these other court rulings. Enlighten us as to why now.

BOOKER: You know, we had a terrible fight in the Senate. And — and I love my 46 Democratic colleagues. But it was — if you were in those caucus meetings when we were debating over what to do with the continuing resolution, everybody who stood up actually had principled, strong feelings. But we were divided against each other on what to do. And I think I felt so, I won't use the word demoralized, but pretty down after that. And then I got it from people around my state who were just fit to be tied.

And it really — that was a time that it really came together for me, that I — enough is enough. I've been doing a role in my caucus right now of I believe in the people that I serve with, so I've been trying to get them to lean into social media where more and more of our voters are. And I've been proud of them. But I said, I — I need to step out and do something. And so I — I was hoping I could do something that would unify our caucus and unify people who believe that the country is facing really disastrous waters right now. And so I figured this was it.

And I'll have to say, I — I didn't know if I could last five hours...

MELBER: Sure.

BOOKER: ... 10 hours, 15 hours. But I always knew that out there, way out there, hoping it was within my reach, was the fact that a fierce segregationist was a guy that in this body, this noble body, hasn't always been right, has often been wrong, but over our history, I think the arc of our nation has bent more towards justice. But I thought it was an injustice that that record was held by that segregationist.

MELBER: You mentioned the segregationist Strom Thurmond. That has been part of the Senate history. If you read the Robert Caro books and the way the Senate rules were sometimes twisted to further the hand of segregation, and then, interestingly, sometimes the Senate rules allowed for progress, which is why we got, as you know, and I think viewers know, Civil Rights Act of '64 and the Voting Rights Act of '65. He was fighting against that progress. And so I briefly want to play a little bit about him, because you did break his record yesterday. Take a look.

(BEGIN VIDEO CLIP)

UNINDENTIFIED: James Strom Thurmond of South Carolina was known for one thing race, his opposition to integration in America.

SEN. STROM THURMOND (D), SOUTH CAROLINA: And I tell you, the American people from one side or the other had had better wake up and oppose such a program!

UNINDENTIFIED: He holds the all time filibuster record in the Senate. He once talked for 24 hours straight. The subject was civil rights.

(END VIDEO CLIP)

MELBER: He was on the wrong side of history, do you think some, not all, perhaps, but some of what Donald Trump is doing and those who acquiesce, who are quiet, or those billionaires who once criticized him and now join him, as a legal resident father is shipped away, are they on the wrong side of history?

BOOKER: Well, you know, you're making me emotional looking at that because while he was in the Senate, a place that Black people were never imagined to be, a brief period in Reconstruction in the 1800s, my family, my elders, were suffering at the hands of that kind of leadership. And, you know, my parents raised my brother and I in a very privileged, amazing town in New Jersey. But segregationists wouldn't let us move in. My parents had to get a white couple in 1969 to pose as my parents to put a bid on the house that was eventually accepted, and the closing came about.

And, you know, my dad and mom were very clear to my brother and I growing up in this relative privilege for two Black boys, to let us know whose shoulders we stand on, who bled, who sweat, who struggled so that we could have the opportunities. And my parents, if anything they commanded my brother and I to do, was to prove worthy of all those struggles and sacrifices.

So my mom, who was watching from her TV set, who did a sit-in in Charlotte, North — in Charlotte, Virginia, excuse me, who told me about the indignities and the suffering of growing up in a world that men like him so viciously segregated, there was a deep satisfaction in that, that — that thread that — that I hold on to, that is still being held by those ancestors who fought against that.

And so for me, that compelled me. I tell you, when — when members of the Congressional Black Caucus, who are my elders, some of them, some of them are much younger than me, but they stretch the gamut, when they started pouring into that chamber as I got closer and closer to that segregationist record, I just felt really lifted by them. I looked into the galleries and saw so many younger people than me, and then knew that we could change history together, not just in one guy's speech, but just like all those people who brought about the Civil Rights Act, who brought about the Voting Rights Act, that brought about the Fair Housing Act of '68 that really was the law that we had on our side to buy the home we bought. That we maybe could prove worthy of those blessings collectively.

And so when you bring up Donald Trump and some of the history of his family, I — I don't want to demonize him, because John Lewis and other marchers who stood up against the worst of the worst, they never, never gave in to hate. They never gave in to diminishing their character in order to aggrandize their own. But they talked about the issues. And I hope America right now doesn't get too caught up in making Donald Trump the center of their focus, because that's what he wants to be.

I want people to return to the center of their focus, the people that might lose their Medicaid, the people that might lose their Social Security, the veterans right now who are being fired this — for this man who were doing noble work in their continuation of service and helping other veterans. We've got to center those people, elevate our conversation and deepen our fight.

I've learned a hard lesson as a Democrat. We've made a lot of mistakes. We were — we've let a lot of people down. But we've got to learn. And — and I think what people want, as you said, they just want us — they want to know that we hurt and that we're angry, but that we're using that pain and that anger not to consume us or not to turn us into what we're against, but to — to use it as fuel to fight for people.

MELBER: Right. And people have seen versions of this, including protests. I don't want to minimize a lot of what's been going on, but no one's catalyzed this fight who's been in the Democratic Caucus or leadership like this until you this week. So when you hear the talk of — I know — I know you, so I know how you're going to potentially be classy and pivot. But — but there's talk about whether you should be Senate leader for the Democrats. Are you open to that position in the future or running for president again, while we see both the nation and part of the world say, oh, maybe this Cory Booker guy is the new face?

BOOKER: Well, the Senate Democrats have a leader and he's going to remain there. I have an election. New Jersey's got two big elections. We're like Virginia, we've got this November.

MELBER: Yep.

BOOKER: And then I have my re-election in '26. And — and that's why I've got this big state of New Jersey. I want New Jersey to know what my focus is and my priorities are, what they elected me to do. I'm fighting for them. I'll be back in my state doing rallies and town halls this weekend around the issues.

But I want to just challenge you to let you know that maybe here in the Senate, a lot of us have — of my colleagues and I, I've watched them fight. I've watched their grit. I see some of the most amazing human beings. They came out last night for me, whether it was Chris Murphy, who stood with me that entire time, was in the Senate chambers, to Tammy Duckworth, riding in in a wheelchair, lost her legs in combat, to people out there. There's a woman in Iowa named Konfrst, who's been leading — she became a friend of mine when I did run for president. That woman is a fighter. And America may not be paying attention to her, but she is extraordinary. I could...

(CROSSTALK)

MELBER: Well, see...

BOOKER: ... all the states that I know.

MELBER: See, I can say this because — because we've had these conversations over the years, Senator, this is the classy deflection I was talking about.

BOOKER: Right.

MELBER: Because that's great. That's great, substantively. But there, again, my job is to talk to you and everyone else who comes on the show. There are a lot of folks who are saying, OK, Senator Schumer, you had your run, that's great. He's invited on the program. I've had him before. But as you know, we're not going to hide the ball here. There's a lot of conversations about whether over the next few years there should be new leadership.

Before I let you go. You were on this program a while back and we got to the end of the interview. You may not remember, especially after what you've been through this week. And you said, wait, where's my lyric? And I said, well, it's...

(LAUGHTER)

MELBER: I said, it's such a serious day, as it sometimes is, I didn't — we didn't get to one. Well, I brought you one tonight, OK?

BOOKER: OK.

MELBER: Mase, who you remember, "Harlem World," M-A-$-E, on a track featuring the late, great DMX, "If you had 24 hours to live, what would you do? And now I think we know the answer when it comes to you."

BOOKER: Wow. That's deep. Well, I want you to know, before I went out there, there's a great gospel song called "Stand." And it was one of the ones that I use to empower myself. And...

MELBER: What was it called again, "Stand"?

BOOKER: It's called "Stand," S-T-A-N-D.

MELBER: "Stand."

BOOKER: "Stand," yes.

MELBER: And that was your pump up music?

BOOKER: That was one. I lean on a lot of gospel, my brother, to — to stand up and to let me — myself know that my body was not capable of 25 hours, but I knew if I could tap into the spirit, and I think a lot of the spirit that binds us all together, that I can make it through. And so I'm just thankful to all the people that believed and sent me energy and a lot of people who showed up who could.

MELBER: Yep.

BOOKER: And I was able to stand.

MELBER: That is right on point. I love that it's gospel, given some of the themes you shared, both in the speech and tonight. And "Stand," for all those listening or watching around the country, is on Senator Booker's Spotify playlist, apparently. So we always love learning about the playlists.

Senator, you've been very busy. I'm glad you got a seat after all that time standing. Thanks for joining us tonight.

BOOKER: Thank you. Thank you, brother. Thank you, I appreciate you always.

MELBER: Thank you. Senator Cory Booker, the man in the news, the man of the hour, 25 hours, our thanks.

END

data.title: Australia's Service Business Activity Growth Hits Seven-Month High in March
data.description:
Australia's service business activity hit a seven-month high in March, with the seasonally adjusted S&P Global Australia Services PMI Business Activity Index rising to 51.6 from 50.8 in February, according to the results of S&P Global's survey released Thursday.

Growth in new business accelerated at the fastest pace in almost three years due to better economic conditions, marketing efforts, and growing client interest, the report said.

However, export orders declined due to disruptions from cyclone Alfred.

Job creation grew at the fastest pace in nearly two years, the report added.

The Composite Output Index rose to 51.6 in March from 50.6 in February, marking the fastest pace since August 2024 and a sixth straight increase in business activity.

Fuel Prices Fall After Trump Talks Tariffs
data.description:
By Ryan Dezember

Oil, gasoline and diesel prices are dropping following President Trump's unveiling of his tariff plan, reflecting energy traders' concerns that fuel demand will decline as import taxes slow economic activity.

Benchmark U.S. crude futures recently traded below $70 a barrel, down more than 2%, or about $2, from energy markets' afternoon settlement price. Gasoline and diesel futures in New York each dropped by more than 3%.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: U.S. Effective Tariff Rate Highest in 131 YearsMarket Talk
data.description:
1909 ET - President Trump's country-specific reciprocal tariffs turned out to be bigger than expected. The announcements imply the effective tariff rate on all imports will rise to around 26% in 2025 from 2.3% last year, leaving it at a 131-year high, says Neil Shearing, Group Chief Economist at Capital Economics. In very general terms, Canada and Mexico have gotten off lightly, while nations in Asia, particularly China and Vietnam, have been hit hard, he adds. The European Union and Japan sit somewhere in the middle, Shearing said. (james.glynn@wsj.com; @JamesGlynnWSJ)

1856 ET - It was a machine gun spray from President Trump on tariffs so everyone caught a few bullets. Australia will have a 10% tariff on all exports to the U.S., so when compared to China and Europe it could have been worse, says Peter Jolly, the former head of global economic research at National Australia Bank. The key theme about tariffs is that currency markets will move to offset their impact in both countries, but an upward drift in goods prices still seems unavoidable, Jolly says. The big worry is whether businesses hold back investment and employment decisions due to uncertainty around global trade policy, he adds. (james.glynn@wsj.com; @JamesGlynnWSJ)

1856 ET - U.S. equity futures have fallen more than 2.3% following President Trump's far more hawkish than expected tariff announcements. The tariff rates far exceed baseline expectations, and if they aren't negotiated down promptly, expectations for a recession in the U.S. will rise dramatically, says Tony Sycamore, market analyst at IG. Markets are very likely to see Goldman Sachs revise higher their 12-month estimate of a recession in the U.S. from 35% to 50%, he adds. U.S. equity markets aren't priced for a recession, and in the event of a recession in the U.S., stock markets could easily fall another 10%-15% below their mid-March lows, he adds. (james.glynn@wsj.com; @JamesGlynnWSJ)

1850 ET - Economists at Piper Sandler write that the newly announced tariffs are more dramatic and darker for the U.S. economic outlook than they had expected. Piper Sandler's Nancy Lazar and Jake Oubina expects that tariffs will raise U.S. prices by 2.6 percentage points, with a significant corresponding negative hit to economic growth. Piper Sandler cuts its 2Q GDP forecast to minus 1%, from flat. "Given where the big tariff hikes are concentrated--China, Vietnam, Taiwan, etc.--the risks to consumer inflation are much higher than initially thought," the firm writes. (matt.grossman@wsj.com, @mattgrossman)(matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

1842 ET - President Trump's tariff announcement is "worse than the worst case scenario" the street was fearing, say Wedbush analysts Daniel Ives and Scott Devitt. While many details are to be worked out, investors will focus on specifics in the next 24 hours. The jaw-dropper was the 34% reciprocal tariff on China, followed by 32% on Taiwan, along with EU at 20%, they say in a note. Tech stocks will be under pressure due to concerns about demand destruction, supply chain disruptions, and particularly the China-Taiwan aspect of the tariffs, they say. (venkat.pr@wsj.com)

1812 ET - The Trump administration's decision to largely exempt Canada from its tariff plan could set a path for "real negotiation" toward a new economic-and-security pact between the U.S. and its northern neighbor, says the head of Canada's largest business-lobby group. Candace Laing, president of the Canadian Chamber of Commerce, says the world "is waking up today to a reality that Canada has been living with for months." She says the chain reaction of new U.S. tariffs will have severe repercussions on Americans, Canadians and the global economy. President Trump has repeatedly promised hefty 25% on most nonenergy Canadian imports. In a surprise, his tariff order brings no additional new duties on Canadian imports outside of what was previously announced ahead of Wednesday. (paul.vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

1748 ET - Steep new tariffs on Southeast Asian countries will sting manufacturers that moved operations there in order to avoid previous rounds of U.S. tariffs against China. U.S. imports from Thailand, for instance, grew by 12.5% last year to $63.3 billion, and imports from Vietnam grew by 19% year over year to hit $136.6 billion. According to President Trump's tariff chart, the U.S. will now charge a 46% tariff against Vietnam and a 36% tariff against Thailand. (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

1740 ET - The USD's reaction to the announcement of U.S. reciprocal tariffs has been mixed so far, and is expected to develop further through coming hours as the trading day in Asia gets underway and the policy changes are better understood, currency traders say. The U.S. dollar index is current weaker and trading at around 103.71. The conversation about tariffs is not at an end, says Kristina Clifton, currency strategist at CBA. Countries may negotiate to have tariffs delayed or lowered, she says. If the tariffs are seen to pose large disruptions to U.S. business or consumers, then Trump may lower the tariffs or simply not collect them, Clifton adds. (james.glynn@wsj.com; @JamesGlynnWSJ)

1716 ET - The Mexican peso reverses course and strengthens against the U.S. dollar as USMCA partners Mexico and Canada are absent from the list of countries facing additional U.S. reciprocal trade tariffs. "This is good news, above all because of the exemptions made for goods exported to the U.S. under the USMCA," Banco Base's head of analysis Gabriela Siller says in a post on X. Mexico is included in global U.S. tariffs on steel and aluminum, and President Trump said a 25% tariff on all foreign-made automobiles will take effect at midnight. The peso trades at 20.16 to the U.S. dollar versus 20.35 Tuesday. The Mexican stock market's benchmark IPC index closes up 0.9%. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1714 ET - Fed governor Adriana Kugler wants to see upside risks to inflation abate before the Fed returns to cutting interest rates. Speaking at Princeton University, she notes that several measures of expected inflation have increased, and observes that while housing-services inflation has been improving, there have been setbacks in core-goods inflation and market-based services inflation. "I will support maintaining the current policy rate for as long as these upside risks to inflation continue, while economic activity and employment remain stable," Kugler say. "Going forward, I will carefully assess incoming data, the evolving outlook, and changes in the balance of risks." (matt.grossman@wsj.com, @mattgrossman)

1703 ET - Treasury yields give away gains and fall in late trade following Trump's tariff announcements. Stock futures and dollar indexes also decline. The President Trump says the U.S. will impose a 10% across-the-board tariff on all imports, plus reciprocal levies on several countries. Upcoming data will likely be watched for signs of how trade wars will impact consumer prices and economic growth. Some analysts expect the clarification about tariffs to bringcalm to Wall Street and rekindle animal spirits. The 10-year yield is at 4.145% and the two-year at 3.860%. The WSJ Dollar Index falls 0.3% and the ICE US Dollar gauge is down 0.8%. S&P 500 futures fall 1.4%. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1655 ET - Markets are discounting the possibility of near-term Fed rate cuts after President Trump's tariff announcement. The implied odds of a quarter-point cut in May have decreased to 13%, from 19% before the announcement. The implied odds that the Fed won't cut rates at all through June has climbed to 31%, from 24% pre-announcement. Yet traders are still showing a strong conviction that the Fed will return to cutting rates before the end of 2025, with expectations centered on the possibilities of one, two or three cuts this year. (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering U.S. politics. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1901 ET - U.S. President Trump's tariff announcement has "caused some impressive gyrations in markets," IG's Chris Beauchamp says in an email. "The full range of tariffs deals a remarkable blow to the global trading system, as the U.S. enacts steep charges on its main trading partners," the chief market analyst say. "This scenario is far worse than had been anticipated. After a brief relief rally (overnight), the outlook for equity markets seems grim," Beauchamp says. This news has sent equity futures sharply lower, Beauchamp adds. eMini NASDAQ 100 futures last down 4.3%; eMini S&P 500 futures last 3.5% lower; eMini Dow futures last 2.4% down. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

1859 ET - Former USTR Carla Hills calls President Trump's new tariff regime "crazy policy" and "tremendously harmful." Hills, who was the chief trade official under President George H.W. Bush, says the U.S. and global economy is now in a period of elevated uncertainty. "This incredulous trade action will have a very adverse effect here in the U.S.," Hills told the Canadian Broadcasting Corp. She adds that she fears for the future of North American trade. She helped finalize the original NAFTA trade pact, which was later renegotiated during Trump's first term and renamed USMCA. (paul.vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

1857 ET - Wayfair and other furniture companies shifted sourcing to a variety of Asian countries after President Trump's first term posed steep levies on China, the company's Chief Executive Niraj Shah said on an earnings call in February. "There's sources inside Asia, so places like Cambodia, Indonesia, Thailand, Philippines and Vietnam for sure, that have grown as places where folks have factories and where our goods are coming from," Shah told analysts at the time. The stock's fall, down 15% in after-hours trading, suggests its efforts to shift its supply chain could now be in vain as Trump's new tariffs promise some of the biggest levies on several of its now-key suppliers, including Indonesia and Vietnam, with levies of 32% and 46%, respectively.(kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1838 ET - RH has been further diversifying its manufacturing network since the company realized China was going to be a long term problem, CEO Gary Friedman says on a call with analysts. "Now it looks like moving out of China didn't make a difference," the executive says. The game has changed and it doesn't matter where you go, except for America and the U.S., where the company has been expanding its capabilities, he says. "It's going to be messy, but it's going to be messy for everyone," Friedman says, while acknowledging that he doesn't like to be caught off guard. Overall, RH can navigate the current environment "pretty well" over the short term, the executive adds. RH shares fall 25% to $186 in post-market trading. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

1831 ET - VF, which owns brands including North Face, Vans and Timberland, has been waging a turnaround. President Trump's sweeping new tariff plan that calls for the U.S. to impose a 10% tariff on all imports and much higher rates for some nations, will likely make its revitalization efforts more difficult. During its fiscal 2024, VF's products were sourced from independent manufacturers primarily located in Asia, the company said in its most recent annual filing with the Securities and Exchange Commission. A slew of events, including 34% duties on China, could hurt the company's ability to source or deliver products, as well as its profitability, it says. Shares fall 5.2% in late trading. If the tariffs are seen to pose large disruptions to U.S. business or consumers, then Trump may lower the tariffs or simply not collect them, Clifton adds. (james.glynn@wsj.com; @JamesGlynnWSJ)

1735 ET - Lululemon Athletica thought it was safe from President Trump's tariffs. That wasn't the case. The Vancouver-based company doesn't make its yoga pants and running shorts in Canada, which previously gave it a competitive advantage over other Canadian companies that do manufacture domestically, such as Canada Goose. The company, however, produces nearly 90% of its athletic gear in Vietnam, Cambodia, Sri Lanka, Indonesia and Bangladesh. Shares fall 12% in late trading after President Trump announced reciprocal tariffs of 46% on Vietnam, 49% on Cambodia, 44% on Sri Lanka, 32% on Indonesia and 37% on Bangladesh. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

data.title: Call for President's Rule for Singapore Before GE2025: “Global Citizens' Manifesto” Exposes Singapore’s Political Reality
data.description:
The book "Global Citizens' Manifesto: Happy New Democracy" by Singaporean author EShan V challenges Singapore's political system, proposing AI-enhanced governance to replace pseudo-democracy. It advocates for President's Rule before the 2025 elections and promotes direct dynamic democracy over regency republics.

SINGAPORE, April 02, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) — A Singaporean in his sixties has joined forces with artificial intelligence (AI) to craft a bold democratic model that challenges Singapore's political structure. His work, "Global Citizens' Manifesto: Happy New Democracy," demonstrates how partnerships with AI can create practical alternatives to existing governance systems.

Singapore, long criticized for tight political controls, restricted media freedom, and notorious for gaslighting people through mass media, now faces a compelling alternative through this publication. "Global Citizens' Manifesto" presents the first comprehensive blueprint for democratic reform, tailored explicitly to small nations yet applicable worldwide, with Singapore positioned as an ideal testing ground.

"Our current democratic systems have fundamental flaws that prevent true representation. AI offers us tools to correct these imbalances and return political power to citizens in meaningful ways," states EShan, creator of "Global Citizens' Manifesto."

The manifesto calls for President's Rule in Singapore before the GE2025 elections while the UN conducts a “Democracy Compliance Check,” arguing that the current system represents a pseudo-democracy that requires fundamental restructuring. It proposes a direct dynamic democracy model to replace what EShan terms the “Regency Republic,” which has dominated democracies worldwide.

The book introduces industry/profession-based constituency structures for democratic nations, ensuring that governance reflects diverse expertise across economic sectors. This structure replaces what EShan calls an “Elected Oligarchy,” where electoral mechanisms create an illusion of representation while concentrating power among elites.

The publication is critical for Singapore's citizens, particularly regarding financial autonomy. Recent changes to Singapore's Central Provident Fund have greatly concerned the author, as the government has introduced a retirement account (RA) system that effectively restricts citizens from accessing over $200,000 of their life savings. With Provident Fund contributions being the highest in the world, EShan now doubts the safety of these funds and whether the government has devised a scheme to manage withdrawals using fresh inflows, all while maintaining its confidence to win elections indefinitely.

"As Abraham Lincoln famously noted, you cannot fool all the people all the time," EShan says. "The absence of meaningful opposition questioning these fundamental changes to citizens' financial freedoms reveals the staged nature of Singapore's multi-party system."

This manifesto pushes beyond theoretical critique by outlining a decentralized governance system supported by AI capabilities. The proposed framework moves past the limitations of representative democracy toward direct citizen participation in policy formation and implementation.

Released amid growing global conversations about democracy's resilience and technology's applications in governance, this book showcases how human insight, combined with machine intelligence, can tackle complex political challenges that have frustrated reformers for generations.

The author is currently engaged in translating his work into all languages worldwide. Publishers interested in collaborating with the author can send publishing proposals directly via email, mentioning their language of choice.

"Global Citizens' Manifesto: Happy New Democracy" is available on Kindle for readers interested in exploring its arguments and proposals. The timing coincides with increasing demands for democratic renewal, offering concrete steps toward governance systems built on technological innovation and substantive citizen involvement.

The companion volume, "Global Citizens' Manifesto. Solution to World Water Crisis: The Culprit that Causes Water Crisis, Identified," explores a critical challenge facing humanity. It examines how the outdated siphon toilet system contributes to excessive water waste and presents innovative “Virtual Siphon Toilet” for conservation and sustainable sanitation solutions.

data.title: Canada, Mexico Get Short-Term Advantage From Trump's Tariff MovesMarket Talk
data.description: Desjardins Group economist Royce Mendes says President Trump's new tariff policy, as it applies to Canada, reinforces the firm's view that the Bank of Canada will remain on hold on April 16, or its next rate-policy announcement. Canada and Mexico will face no new duties, other than what was previously announced prior to Wednesday. Mendes says this gives Canada and Mexico "some short-term advantage" over other countries. As a result there is some scope for the two countries to take market share from other U.S. trading partners. Mendes warns that while there remain unresolved trade irritants between the U.S. and Canada, traders might interpret the Liberation Day moves as a starting point for negotiations on a new North American trade pact. data.title: Jiangsu Zenergy Battery Technologies Is Engaged in Manufacturing on Lithium-Ion Battery
Canada, Mexico not subject to new global rates as fentanyl tariff still in place
data.description:
Mexico and Canada avoided fresh tariffs on Wednesday with President Donald Trump exempting the United States' top trading partners from his new 10% global tariff baseline, although previous duties remain in place.

Goods from Mexico and Canada that comply with the USMCA trade agreement between the three countries will largely remain exempt from tariffs, except for auto exports and steel and aluminum which fall under separate tariff policies.

Trump previously imposed 25% tariffs on Mexico and Canada for not doing enough to curb migration and fentanyl trafficking, but later issued a carve out for USMCA compliant goods.

"For Canada and Mexico, the existing fentanyl/migration ... orders remain in effect, and are unaffected by this order," the White House fact sheet said.

"In the event the existing fentanyl/migration ... orders are terminated, USMCA compliant goods would continue to receive preferential treatment, while non-USMCA compliant goods would be subject to a 12% reciprocal tariff."

Some analysts said Canada and Mexico appeared to have avoided the worst-case scenario.

“Mexico and Canada are almost certainly sighing relief after today’s announced tariffs," said Michael Camunez, chief executive of Monarch Global Strategies, which advises firms doing business in Mexico.

"The North American partners were shielded from what clearly could have been a very bad day.”

Candace Laing, president and CEO of the Canadian Chamber of Commerce, said in a statement: "We hope that today's positioning regarding Canada by the U.S. is part of a path to real negotiation, ultimately leading to long-term partnership."

Speaking after Trump's announcement, Canada's Prime Minister Mark Carney said he still planned to respond with countermeasures.

"We are going to fight these tariffs with countermeasures, we are going to protect our workers, and we are going to build the strongest economy in the G7 (group of nations)," he said.

data.title: Trump tariffs tip world into trade war abyss
data.description:
The president shocked US trade partners with a minimum 10% tariff and higher rates on everyone from China and the EU to Vietnam. His irreconcilable goals of raising $6 trln in revenue while reshoring manufacturing will complicate negotiations – and accelerate a deeper crisis.

Full view will be published shortly.

Follow @Rubinations on X

CONTEXT NEWS

U.S. President Donald Trump said on April 2 that the United States would impose a 10% baseline tariff on all imports and higher rates on many of the country's biggest trading partners.

While calling the duties “our declaration of economic independence” during an announcement in the White House Rose Garden, Trump held up a poster listing some of the individual rates, including 34% on China, 20% on the European Union and 46% on Vietnam, as a response to levies imposed on U.S. goods.

The baseline 10% tariff is set to take effect on April 5, according to a subsequent statement from the White House, and the higher rates will start on April 9.

The president is also planning other tariffs targeting semiconductors, pharmaceuticals, and potentially critical minerals, a White House official, speaking on condition of anonymity, told Reuters.

Mexico and Canada, two of the largest U.S. trading partners, were not included on the newly released list, but already face 25% tariffs on many of their goods.

data.title: How Trump's New Tariffs Will Impact Countries Differently
data.description:
By Gavin Bade

Trump added onto an already complex tariff picture. Here are some examples of how new levies announced Wednesday will impact countries differently:

  • China is essentially in its own bucket. Trump, since beginning his second term, had already placed 20% additional tariffs on imports from the nation. So his move to slap an additional 34% in duties on Chinese imports brings their total to 54% after April 9. If Trump ends up imposing additional 25% tariffs on China for purchasing Venezuelan oil, then the tariff rate would bump up to 79%.
  • Trump planned to impose 25% tariffs on most goods from Canada and Mexico not for unfair trade practices, but for what he said was their role in the fentanyl crisis and illegal immigration into the U.S. Canada and Mexico wouldn't immediately be subject to the new tariff regime, senior administration officials told reporters Wednesday, but instead would still be subject to the previous levies. The administration gave an exemption to those tariffs for cars and other products compliant with the U.S.-Mexico-Canada Agreement. But those exemptions expire on April 2, and the White House didn't announce a further extension. Energy products from Canada will continue to be tariffed at 10%.
  • Then there are about 60 nations the White House considers bad actors on trade. The administration is looking to penalize them for their own tariffs and non-tariff barriers like regulations and taxes that affect U.S. firms. The European Union will get a 20% levy, Vietnam will get 46%, Taiwan will get 32%, India will get 26% and Japan 24%, among other nations.
  • And then there's virtually every other nation. The administration is imposing a baseline 10% tariff on all other imports.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Chips and Dip for Dinner? These Fresher, Healthier Recipes Make Retro Snacks Mealworthy
data.description:
By Georgia Freedman

Growing up in Southern California in the 1980s, the thing I looked forward to most at parties wasn't a game or a goody bag. It was the ubiquitous tub of dip made with Lipton onion soup mix. From the packet of "chemical" flavoring that made the sour cream so intensely tasty to the fatty, salty chips on the side, everything about this snack flew in the face of the brown-rice-and-tofu dinners, carob desserts and organic everything I was raised on.

The 1980s were a halcyon era for snacking. Cool Ranch Doritos. Boboli pizzas. Lunchables. But my parents were hippies who shopped at a natural-food coop; we got fruit leather. Predictably, the snacks I was denied assumed an aspirational aura.

Creating recipes for my new book, " Snacking Dinners," took me right back to those '80s cravings. Older now, with eating habits far closer to my parents' than my child self ever would have predicted, I wondered: Could I make healthier, tastier versions of my childhood favorites without robbing them of joy?

My snack revisionism had actually begun when my own daughter reached elementary-school age and my life became, once again, all about snacks: to pack in her lunches, to eat in the car after school, to take on weekend trips. Snacks even served as dinner when we needed something fast, easy and appealing to all the age groups at our table.

Ants-on-a-log got a remix with Parmesan, anchovies and olives (inspired by an appetizer at San Francisco's Zuni Café). I made a fresh take on potato skins by halving boiled baby potatoes and setting up a plate of mix-and-match toppings like grated cheese, chopped pickles and herbs, and minced salami or jerky.

I'll give my parents this: What they didn't allow us in the way of "junk food," they more than made up for with Indian dishes from Madhur Jaffrey's books and yellowtail nigiri from our local sushi spot. I drew on those memories, too, to punch up the flavor with global ingredients. My years living in China's Yunnan province resurfaced, surprisingly, in my queso recipe, with fresh mango for sweetness, chile crisp for heat and Sichuan peppercorn oil for a nice numbing tingle.

With my old crush, chips and dip, I opted for sweet potato chips this time around, then drizzled on a feta-yogurt dressing in Pollock-like squiggles, and scattered fresh herbs and za'atar spice mix all over.

The child in me was more than satisfied.

data.title: Excellon Advances Offtake Financing for Mallay Mine Restart
data.description:
Toronto, Ontario--(Newsfile Corp. - April 2, 2025) - Excellon Resources Inc. (FSE: E4X2) ("Excellon" or the "Company") is pleased to report good progress toward securing offtake-linked financing for the restart of operations at the past-producing Mallay Silver Mine ("Mallay") in Peru.

Excellon has received several non-binding proposals from leading trading houses for zinc and lead concentrate offtake agreements. Each proposal includes an associated prepayment or structured finance facility to provide near-term funding for restart activities at Mallay.

The proposals received contemplate:

  • Financing facilities ranging from US$4 to US$6 million.

  • Tenors of up to 30 months, including grace periods during ramp-up.

  • Annual interest rates range from SOFR* + 5% to 7%.

* Secured Overnight Financing Rate

All proposals are subject to customary conditions precedent, including satisfactory due diligence and completion of the Company's previously announced acquisition of Minera CRC S.A.C. ("Minera CRC"), the owner of Mallay. Offtake terms include multi-year commitments for 100% of production.

Shawn Howarth, President and CEO commented: "The interest we've received from globally recognized trading partners for Mallay concentrate validates our strategy to restart Mallay and reinforces our confidence in the project's potential. These proposals provide competitive terms and flexible structures to meet our capital requirements while forming long-term commercial partnerships. We're highly encouraged by the momentum and expect to finalize a preferred financing solution in the near term."

Excellon aims to close the acquisition of Minera CRC in April. The Company expects to conclude offtake discussions coincident with closing of the acquisition.

For Further Information, Please Contact:

Excellon Resources Inc.

Shawn Howarth, President & Chief Executive Officer

info@excellonresources.com

data.title: Trade and labor associations, analysts on Trump's reciprocal tariffs
data.description:
President Donald Trump said on Wednesday he would impose a 10% baseline tariff on all imports to the United States and higher duties on some of the country's biggest trading partners, a move that could escalate a trade war and upend the global economy.

Trading partners are expected to respond with countermeasures that could lead to dramatically higher prices for everything from bicycles to wine.

Trump has already imposed 25% levies on autos and car parts.

LIZ SHULER, PRESIDENT, AMERICAN FEDERATION OF LABOR AND CONGRESS OF INDUSTRIAL ORGANIZATIONS

"The Trump administration's attacks on trade union workers' rights at home, gutting of the government agency that works to discourage the outsourcing of American jobs and efforts to erode critical investments in U.S. manufacturing take us backward."

RICHARD CAPPETTO, SENIOR DIRECTOR OF NORTH AMERICAN GOVERNMENT AFFAIRS, IPC

"A strong U.S. electronics industry requires a comprehensive approach - one that pairs targeted investments and incentives with policies that enhance mutually beneficial trade partnerships. Trade is essential to supply chain resilience, innovation, and cost competitiveness. Without it, tariffs risk raising costs for American businesses and further driving production offshore."

ZOLTAN VAN HEYNINGEN, EXECUTIVE DIRECTOR OF THE U.S. LUMBER COALITION

"We welcome the measures taken by President Trump and his administration's focus on Canada's unfair trade practices. We especially welcome the president's initiation of the Section 232 investigation under the Trade Expansion Act of 1962 focusing on softwood lumber imports."

MARK COMPTON, EXECUTIVE DIRECTOR OF THE AMERICAN EXPLORATION & MINING ASSOCIATION

"We are encouraged by the Trump administration prioritizing domestic mineral production and processing so we have the raw materials a revived manufacturing base and our society requires. We look forward to working with the administration to ensure the domestic mining industry is able to meet that challenge."

TONY REDONDO, FOUNDER AT COSMOS CURRENCY EXCHANGE

"Semiconductor giants like Nvidia face cost hikes from imported chips; Intel may gain but isn't immune. China's retaliation on rare materials could worsen shortages."

"PC makers (Dell, HP) could face 10%-25% cost increases, adding $200-$500/unit, pushing prices up or margins down."

"AI server firms (Nvidia, Amazon) may see delays and millions in extra costs from pricier chips and steel."

"Retailers like Walmart and construction could also suffer."

"Short-term: higher costs, chaos. Long-term: maybe more U.S. manufacturing, but labor and infrastructure lag."

"Consumers face pricier goods by late 2025 unless companies absorb costs - which is rare."

BERNSTEIN ANALYSTS

"We remain concerned that vehicle and parts tariffs are here to stay and will add a substantial cost burden to the sector. If automotive tariffs are not reversed but instead extended, we see further downside risks to automotive stocks."

TOM MADRECKI, VICE PRESIDENT OF SUPPLY CHAIN RESILIENCY, CONSUMER BRANDS ASSOCIATION

"The consumer packaged goods industry already manufactures the majority of its products here in the United States. However, there are critical ingredients and inputs that need to be imported due to scarce availability domestically. No amount of tariffs will bring these inputs back to the U.S."

"Reciprocal tariffs that do not reflect ingredient and input availability concerns will inevitably raise costs, limit consumer access to affordable products and unintentionally harm iconic American manufacturers. We encourage President Trump and his trade advisers to fine-tune their approach and exempt key ingredients and inputs in order to protect manufacturing jobs and prevent unnecessary inflation at the grocery store."

LENNY LAROCCA, KPMG U.S. AUTOMOTIVE LEADER

"U.S. automakers are looking at steps they can take in the shorter term to mitigate tariffs, such as working with suppliers on any items that can be moved to the U.S. quickly without significant investment. But massive longer-term investment decisions require more time and certainty."

"The current U.S. automakers playbook is not enough, and they are facing a watershed moment. It's an opportunity for automakers to do things differently. Lean into emerging technology like AI across their business. Explore and decide on alliances faster. Speed up vehicle production cycle times."

"On the supply side, this watershed moment offers opportunity in the chaos for mergers and acquisitions."

DAVID MCCALL, PRESIDENT, UNITED STEELWORKERS (USW) INTERNATIONAL

"We must ensure our trade policy targets cheaters rather than trusted economic allies like Canada. We should be working to build relationships - not barriers - with partners who have proven their commitment to joining us in tackling global overcapacity."

"The administration must also take steps to prevent companies from using tariffs as an excuse to price gouge consumers."

NIGEL GREEN, CEO, GLOBAL FINANCIAL ADVISORY FIRM DEVERE GROUP

"This is how you sabotage the world's economic engine while claiming to supercharge it."

"It's a seismic day for global trade. Trump is blowing up the post-war system that made the U.S. and the world more prosperous, and he's doing it with reckless confidence."

"Tariffs are taxes - plain and simple - and American consumers will bear the brunt."

"The reality is stark, these tariffs will push prices higher on thousands of everyday goods - from phones to food - and that will fuel inflation at a time when it is already uncomfortably persistent."

MIKE HAWES, CEO, UK'S SOCIETY OF MOTOR MANUFACTURERS AND TRADERS

"These tariff costs cannot be absorbed by manufacturers, thus hitting U.S. consumers who may face additional costs and a reduced choice of iconic British brands, whilst UK producers may have to review output in the face of constrained demand."

SETH GOLDSTEIN, MORNINGSTAR ANALYST ON U.S. CHEMICALS

"I see lower volumes as a result of tariffs. The tariffs will likely be passed along to raise price of end-market products to consumers. In turn, I expect we see consumers buy fewer goods."

"Due to the high fixed-cost nature of chemicals production, lower volumes would have an outsized impact on profits, so we could see another year of declining profits if widespread tariffs are implemented. However, many chemical producers make their products in the U.S. to be sold domestically, so we see less of a direct impact."

DAVID FRENCH, EXECUTIVE VP OF GOVERNMENT RELATIONS AT NATIONAL RETAIL FEDERATION

"More tariffs equal more anxiety and uncertainty for American businesses and consumers. Tariffs are a tax paid by the U.S. importer that will be passed along to the end consumer. Tariffs will not be paid by foreign countries or suppliers. We encourage President Trump to hold trading partners accountable and restore fairness for American businesses without creating economic uncertainty and higher prices for American families."

ART WHEATON, DIRECTOR OF LABOR STUDIES, ILR SCHOOL, CORNELL UNIVERSITY

"It will take years and billions of dollars to bring manufacturing jobs from new plants online, whereas expansions at existing factories could happen much sooner. However, companies prioritize stability - frequent policy changes can delay investment decisions as businesses wait for clearer, long-term signals before committing capital."

MICHAEL ASHLEY SCHULMAN, PARTNER AND CIO AT RUNNING POINT CAPITAL ADVISORS

"Possibly, Trump in addition to bringing back manufacturing to the U.S. and leveraging our advanced robotics within our shores for that manufacturing renaissance, may also be trying to truly muck up the Chinese economy and heighten their economic instability. 34% tariffs on Chinese goods could either force many Chinese manufacturers to close, increasing unemployment and social instability in China, and/or force a temporary price level adjustment within the U.S."

"For chips, PCs, semiconductors and server manufacturers, these tariffs, if they hold, will be quite disruptive."

"Investors, analysts, politicians, and the market will be on bated breath to see what is now negotiated between countries following this 'Liberation Day' volley from the administration. Hopefully, today's announcement is a worst case scenario, and any negotiations create improvements from here."

data.title: Cordlife Completes Testing on Low-Risk Cryogenic Tanks
data.description:
Cordlife Group has received full test results for over 200 donated cord blood samples stored in five cryogenic tanks previously flagged for potential temperature excursions, according to a Wednesday filing on the Singapore Exchange.

The stem cell banking firm is conducting a technical analysis of the findings with professional advisers and specialists.
